question.title: Consecutive Logins
question.desc: Find the user IDs of users who have logged in for at least 3 consecutive days.
question.setup: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS logins; CREATE TABLE logins (user_id INTEGER, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (1, '2022-01-01'), (1, '2022-01-02'), (1, '2022-01-03'), (2, '2022-01-02'), (2, '2022-01-03'), (2, '2022-01-04'), (3, '2022-01-01'), (3, '2022-01-05');
question.solution: WITH ranked_logins AS ( SELECT user_id, login_date, login_date - ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS group_id FROM logins ) SELECT DISTINCT user_id FROM ranked_logins GROUP BY user_id, group_id HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;
question.tips: Using window functions can be more efficient than self-joins for consecutive sequence problems.
hints: Consider using window functions to assign a group ID to each sequence of consecutive logins.
You can use the ROW_NUMBER() function to assign a unique number to each row within a partition of the result set.
To identify consecutive logins, you can subtract the row number from the login date, which will give the same result for consecutive days.

tests.setup: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS logins; CREATE TABLE logins (user_id INTEGER, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (4, '2022-02-01'), (4, '2022-02-02'), (4, '2022-02-03'), (5, '2022-02-04'), (5, '2022-02-05');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS logins; CREATE TABLE logins (user_id INTEGER, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (6, '2022-03-01'), (6, '2022-03-02'), (6, '2022-03-03'), (6, '2022-03-04'), (7, '2022-03-05');
